item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. I wonder what happens if someone else tries to do his work. And since they are blocks of flesh, will they rot if stored instead of being given to him?

+1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image could not be sourced, and the second image could not be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My bad, I missed you upthread.

Using volume directly would be a tidy way to side-step the issue. But would require a more significant re-phrasing.
And since OP hasn't made the edits in the last half decade, it doesn't look like this is going to get patched either way.

As a mental exercise; what would be a reasonable volume for a cubic dog?
Animals are mostly water. So the density would be close enough to 1 that we can round it down.
Google tells me that the smallest dog breeds start at about 4.5 kg for a healthy adult.
Which translates to 4.5 liters, or 4500 cm3.
Which would mean a flesh cube at least 16 cm on a side.

But that assume all the mass gets used.
Since he "whittles" them, we should probably account for some material lost as shavings and scrap. So round up to at least 20 cm per side as the smallest reasonable unit of materials.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Again, that's literally what I posted 5 years ago.

Suggested tweak to fix: Packages are cubic, ranging between ten (10) cm and seventy-five (75) cm on a side…

A cube of that (75 cm) size would have a volume of ~422 liters with the maximum weight of 80 kg. That would make the dogs' density close to balsa wood, nowhere near the usual density of living creatures. I assume "10 to 75 liters" would work better.
In fact, we used liters in our translation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ouch!
I didn't notice until seeing this post, and now I can't unsee the problem.
10 cm3 is the same as 10 ml, or a drop over 2 teaspoons of volume.
Even if the flesh cubes were as dense as gold, the weight of one that small would still top out at under 200 grams.

Suggested tweak to fix: Packages are cubic, ranging between ten (10) cm and seventy-five (75) cm on a side…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it, but 10 cubic centimeters is soooo small, especially for something weighing 3 kg. Do you mean 1000 cm3? 10 cm on a side? And 75 cm3 is still soooo small… 7x5x2.2ish, which wouldn't even fit a puppy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm kinda sad that the dogs die. Is it just an overpopulation thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Packages range in size between ten (10) cm3 and seventy-five (75) cm3 and weigh between three (3) and eighty (80) kilograms

Which means they have a density of 300 to 1067 grams per cc. Osmium, the densest material there is, is only ~22 grams per cc. Solid hydrogen, as I found, is around 70 grams per cc.

You either meant liters or cubes with 10..75 cm sides.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Noticed the self-insert, but it actually doesn't matter too much to me. While normally I frown upon self-inserts (or at least have been effectively trained to do so), it's just that the doctor's last name happens to be your username. I honestly considered changing my username to the name of my Foundation researcher, Dr. E. A. D. (he hasn't been mentioned anywhere except one spot that sucked and I should remove it right now).
But yeah. Upvote. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the way the odd pieces fit together in this article— if even a bit were missing, either the cube of flesh or the mail-order process or the way it's carved, it wouldn't quite hold my interest. The lack of malice is important to the tone, too. And I guess puppies are always a plus.

The way that second picture floats in the middle of the page is irritating, though, just stylistically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, we have no idea where the flesh blocks come from or how they are always correctly delivered so as to go wherever the sculptor is housed.

…I kinda want to see them send him to the moon base and see if the mail still gets delivered :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, that was just a very loose after thought. Hopefully subtle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it but did you just write an article around a punnish SCP title?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I sat here for like five minutes highlighting the page over and over to try and see any text. Level up, +2 points to Derping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sure I have no idea what you're talking about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Azzle, I have been wondering for awhile. Why are all your recent posts done in smaller fonts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For many people, most sentient beings would be classified as Euclid- in that were they to want to escape, they could do something about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like this but why is it Euclid class?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bravo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great twist on a mail-based SCP. One quibble with this:

SCP-1787-A is an Italian man

Should be "is a male human, Caucasian" or whatnot. The Italian part comes out later in the description, so we'll still get that info.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I hope the insert wasn't too off putting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I have seen some people don't like the self insert but I don't see it as a big deal as I am not doing anything significant. Thanks again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you. As Azzleflux has said, this isnt my first (The Crowd and The Infectious Time Loop were my other two).

I havent been around/writing much for many months because I was in Hong Kong for four months last year, I never had time.

But I'm glad you like it and I have got a few more ideas in the works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's Pugmalion. Cute! Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not a first

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the tone could be better in some spots, but I enjoyed this overall. I also didn't mind the unobtrusive self-insert. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really, really like this. This is a great first SCP, and I'm looking forward to what you write in the future!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not a fan of the self-insert, but this is pretty well done. I see no reason to downvote an otherwise fine piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got really worried at the start of this. It didn't go where I thought it was going to go, and I like the concept. Good upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The animal shows no sign of pain whilst other parts of its body are being carved and sculpted (and have shown signs of boredom until it is finished.)

For some reason this strikes me as really adorable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weird as hell… I love it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those poor dogs. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoa. I like the concept enough to upvote. The little errors don't bother me much. So +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have clearly been out of the game, so I guess the tonal issues are a huge thing. I shall be going over this with a fine tooth comb should it stay. I gladly welcome any minor changes to fix the tonal issues, I trust people here and have no doubt any changes would make it better.

I appreciate your offer and any help you can provide.

As for right now its almost midnight and I will be going through all the comments in the morning.

Thanks again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
WHAT THE FUUUUUUCK?!

This would be great if it weren't for the tonal issues. If you still need help by Tuesday, I'll be glad to give it to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was actually in the middle of correcting most of those tone issues when you posted this comment. :-P Check again and see if it's better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aaaand edits done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. There were a few problems though. The worst was a self insert in the Containment Procedures, but I was willing to ignore that and upvoted based on the rest of the article. I do have to say this, though, You should not coldpost. Run it by chat first or the forums. Anyways, great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, but, as other have said before me, the tonal issues have kept me from upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
show full review
hide full review

remarked as being an ‘ordinary old man’

Firstly, you don't need this quotation at all. It isn't necessary. This entire thing isn't necessary.

A typical package contents

Actually label as if it was a photo. As in tell us what the picture is supposed to be. "An Photo of Package XXXX-15's Contents" or something. I don't understand the quoted caption.

larger ‘projects’

You don't need quotations here. It has larger projects. Ok. Then don't have these quotes.

Otherwise they are to be kept in a locked drawer in Dr. Scooter’s office.

Let's not contain items related to specific SCPs in personnel's personal offices. It makes very little sense.

when SCP-1787 ‘whittles’

Remove the quotes, find a better word. At this point it sounds like you're using quotes just to try and have an excuse to use poor tone. Except it doesn't work that way. Perhaps call the packages SCP-XXXX-01 or something, and then say 'when SCP-XXXX undergoes construction event' or something.

treated like a normal dog (as far as physically possible)

This is iffy tone. I would think it should be '…treated as closely to a normal canine as possible until completion' or something.

All of SCP-1787’s creations are to be physically and chemically analysed, before being euthanized, samples to be taken and frozen, and the remains incinerated.

This is a butchered sentence. Maybe better as 'All of SCP-XXXX's creations are to be physically and chemically analyzed before being euthanized. Samples are to be taken and frozen; the remains are to be incinerated.' or something similar.

Packages ranges

Packages range*

and weighs

and weigh*

Inside the package is a cube of flesh, usually coated in fur that SCP-1787 uses as a sculpting medium.

This implies that the fur is used as a sculpting medium. Maybe it would be better to reword to make it clear that the sculpting medium is this flesh-cube. Also 'cube of flesh' is very iffy in my opinion.

DNA testing at this stage identifies it to be canine.

A little iffy tone I think. Maybe better as '…identifies it to be of (canis familiaris or some other thing like that)' or something.

SCP-1787 will use a variety of bladed instruments, such as planes, files, knives and chisels

You do not need the first comma.

This can take, depending on the size of the dog and the ‘quality of the material’, between 6 hours and 5 days.

Well, firstly, those quotes again. Also, 'This can take between 6 hours and 5 days, depending on the size of the dog and reported quality of the material'. This way you remove commas and quotes! Of course, this is my opinion and someone else may have a better suggestion.

to carve out a dog
During this process, SCP-1787 will carve out canine features.

Uhm. If it's carving out a dog, no shit it will carve out canine features. I find the second sentence overly redundant.

‘Finished’ sections of SCP-1787s carvings will become animate

You don't need quotations. If they're finished they're damn finished. They're not finished but only kinda finished, or finished but not really finished in the definition of finishing. If it's finished it's finished in the definition of which we know finished to be.

I.e.

This is not how you use i.e.
http://theoatmeal.com/comics/ie
Refer to the following link to learn what i.e. is used for and what you can replace it with so it makes more sense. The comic will portray better than I can explain.

Who provides the packages and how they are made is unknown.

This is a little iffy tone. Maybe it will be better as 'The source of the packages, as well as the method of creating the original cubes, are still unknown' or something. Maybe.

unable to answer beyond that ‘an old friend’ sends it the materials

Ok. This is the only quote that is remotely bearable to me. But just barely. You may want to edit appropriately.


Overall, interesting concept but there are tone and sentence and grammar issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. I would greatly appreciate help with any tonal issues you see. A fresh set of eyes with more experience would be very helpful. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are a lot of minor tonal issues that are bugging me and keeping me from voting. Your 'overuse' of 'quotation' marks is one of those issues. May I have permission to go through and fix those?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a lot better than 99% of cold-posted first SCPs, and for that, I've upvoted… but still, it could use some extra polish.

For example, the cubes and the resultant organisms could be designated SCP-1787-B and SCP-1787-C, and the man himself as SCP-1787-A.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed and thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, while I'm in the neighbourhood:

a domesticated dog to it’s master

Should be "its". I should probably also mention that I find the concept intriguing and have upvoted the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for that catch. I wasn't sure if it needed to be plural as he uses lots of the same thing, or singular because he works on them individually. Though medium reads better. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Inside the package is a cube of flesh, usually coated in fur that SCP-1787 uses as a sculpting media.

"Media" is plural. The singular would be "medium". I'd change it myself, but you seem to be editing the article right now. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, that was mentioned. I have moved them about so they are vertical, not horizontal. Hopefully this makes it look a bit more clear instead of the way it was.

Thanks. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. What do you think? I think it makes it look a lot less cluttered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just love the imagery of this one. It's very surreal to think of a guy carving dogs out of flesh like an artist carves sculptures out of marble.

I do think the images warp the article format a bit, making the containment procedures stretch out so they look longer than they actually are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You cut the entire image info and paste it down the page. If you have two images right next to each other with no next in between, you end with what you have here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to be honest, I'm not entirely sure how, I would if I knew, could you point me in the right direction so I can fix this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pictures where they are look kinda poorly. Move one of then down the page a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting indeed, but a bit of tone errors (such as "ordinary old man".) No vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry about that, missed it. Fixed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting concept, but downvoted due to SCP-XXX still being in there. :T
